items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.

items.q: What is Taylor's theory of scientific management?
items.correct: Workers are motivated primarily by money; efficiency is maximised by breaking tasks into standardised steps, training workers in 'the one best way', and using piece-rate pay.
items.misconception: Taylor said workers are motivated by recognition and teamwork.
items.random_wrong: Taylor invented the assembly line at Ford.
items.why: Students confuse Taylor with Mayo or Maslow. Taylor's view was that workers respond to pay incentives, not social or psychological factors — his theory is famously narrow and mechanistic.

items.correct: Cost advantages a firm gains as it grows — average cost per unit falls because fixed costs spread over more units, bulk discounts, and specialisation become possible.
items.misconception: Economies of scale means total costs decrease as the business grows.
items.random_wrong: Economies of scale means a business saves the economy money.
items.why: Total costs RISE with growth — it's AVERAGE cost per unit that falls. Students confuse total and average, missing the precise meaning of the concept.

items.q: IRR is the rate at which:
items.why: It's NPV equals zero. “Payback equals zero” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: When IRR and NPV conflict for mutually exclusive projects, theorists prefer:
items.why: It's NPV. “IRR” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many appraisal methods are commonly taught (payback, ARR, NPV, IRR)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
